summary: Jeff Atwood argues that making information public rather than keeping it secret is the key to success in the modern web era. He builds on Marc Hedlund's observation that successful web businesses often reimagine old UNIX commands for the web, and Jason Kottke's insight that breakthrough services like Blogger, Flickr, YouTube, and Twitter succeed by making previously private activities public and permanent. Atwood extends this to argue that obscurity is not just unhelpful but irrational—if no one knows about your work, you don't get the full benefit from it. He concludes that in a world of infinitely searchable micro-content, every public contribution enriches everyone, and the default should always be openness.
key_points: Successful web businesses often translate old UNIX commands into web-accessible services (grep→Google, pine→Gmail, wall→Twitter)
The pattern behind Web 2.0 success is taking private activities and making them public and permanent
Companies that embrace transparency and publicize even their failings grow stronger than those clinging to secrecy
Obscurity is not protective—it's a source of powerlessness that prevents you from getting full value from your work
In a world of searchable micro-content, the inclusionists are winning and every contribution enriches the whole
money_quotes: Power, it seems, comes from public information. Secrets are only a source of powerlessness.
If no one knows you did X, did you really get all the benefits for doing X?
Until you've written a blog entry about X, posted Flickr photos of X, uploaded a video of X to YouTube, typed a Twitter message about X... did X really happen at all?
Everything's interesting to someone, even if that someone is only you and a few other people in the world.
We live in a world of infinitely searchable micro-content, and every contribution, however small, enriches all of us.
Obscurity sucks. But obscurity by choice is irrational.
When in doubt, make it public.
key_insight: In the age of searchable micro-content, choosing obscurity over openness is irrational—power and value flow from making your work public, not from keeping it secret.
